specific_location: Devil's Kitchen, Colorado Nat. Monument; see previous description
description: These are my notes from the time of the sighting:  On June 11th, just around noon, I was walking the Devil's Kitchen Trail looking for Gray Vireo. In the area below where the "steps" rise up to the kitchen proper, I heard a Gray Vireo singing. The bird flew from my right to my left and landed up on a shelf and continued singing. I photographed it, then began pishing to try to draw it in closer. Immediately, a Black-chinned Sparrow landed on this same shelf about 30 feet away from me (maybe 5 feet above eye level) in good light and it SANG. It was on a little snag very close to the ground. It was facing right with the tail to the left and looked up towards 2pm when it sang. I was looking with Swarovski 8x32s; The song started with very clear dropping notes and then ended with acclerated notes -- it was almost a two part song -- the liquid clear dropping notes evenly spaced then an accelerated finish. The bird had a black face that extended below the chin; it was generally bluish steel -- bluer than a Gray-headed Junco (and more mask) -- It had brown tones on its back, but not the rich mantle color of a GHJunco. It had a long tail, and acted like an agitated Field Sparrow. I did NOT note bill color. When the bird flew-up, it was a very basic and straightforward sighting; I was like "Cool -- a Black-chinned Sparrow". I then -- SHOOT ME IN THE HEAD -- took two steps closer to try to get a better photo (not thinking how rare this bird might be). It flew off. I then sat there and started thinking to myself that I don't think that these guys are supposed to be here. Still unsure, I called my birding friend back in Illinois and asked him to do a quick check on BChSparrow in Colorado. I checked an iPhone range map, and realized I better get this damn thing photographed. I did see it again -- it popped up right next to a female House Finch (it was longer and more slender than the House Finch), in much worse light, and then quickly flew from the east to the west -- left to right - over to the rocky slopes to the right, where I heard it sing again.  I do not know with what one can confuse a good look at an adult male Black-chinned Sparrow other than maybe a Gray-headed Junco. I know you do not know me, but I started birdwatching when I was three or four (I'm 43 now). I've been coming out west -- usually Wyoming -- almost annually, since 1970 (with a gap in the '90s). I consider myself very familiar with all of the western Juncos. I have birded numerous times in the southwest -- Az,NM, So.Calif. I have seen Black-chinned Sparrows several times in those areas. My ABA list is around 660, and my Colorado list is probably around 180. I only mention these points so you can hopefully have some confidence in the observer. When this bird popped up, there wasn't any question -- light was good, the bird SANG while I was looking at it -- I could have photographed it right then and there -- that was a terrible screw-up on my part.  I returned after an overnight in Ouray and spent two hours up on this shelf and in this immediate area midday without any sign -- visual, song,call.  The habitat here is pretty damn good for this species -- rocky slopes with scrubby shrubs interspersed. I am curious how much coverage this place gets -- this bird acted as if it was on territory -- I think that's why it didn't register at first as a rarity -- he looked like he was exactly where he thought he should be.   Please let me know if you need any other details, if you think I'm missing something, or for any general feedback.  Thanks, everyone.Behaviors: see aboveCall: see belowPlumage: Breeding
